text.title: Card Sorting Process
text.summary: Six-step process for planning and running card sort studies to inform information architecture decisions.

rule.statement: State specific research questions before selecting card sort type or preparing cards.
rule.rationale: Goals determine whether to run open (discover categories), closed (validate structure), or hybrid (refine existing IA) sort. Mixing goals in one study produces inconclusive data.

example.note: Research questions: How do users group our content? What labels make sense to users? Does our navigation match user mental models?

rule.statement: Use 30-60 cards with real content labels written in user language, not internal terminology.
rule.rationale: Over 60 cards causes cognitive fatigue. Jargon on cards tests familiarity with internal terminology rather than mental models. Under 10 items does not justify the study overhead.

list.item: Open sort: users create their own categories — use for early exploration and discovery
Closed sort: users sort into predefined categories — use to validate a proposed structure
Hybrid: predefined + user can add — use to refine an existing IA
In-person: richer discussion, follow-up questions possible, limited scale
Remote/digital: scalable, convenient, less engagement per participant

rule.statement: Recruit 15-20 participants for open sort; 30+ for closed sort statistical validity.
rule.rationale: Below 15 participants, co-occurrence clusters are statistically unstable. Closed sort requires more participants because placement percentages need sufficient N to be meaningful.

rule.statement: Give participants clear, neutral instructions that do not prime any grouping scheme.
rule.rationale: Priming instructions contaminate results. Instructions must state the task without suggesting categories or grouping logic.

example.note: Open sort: "Please organize these cards into groups that make sense to you. You can create as many groups as you need. When done, give each group a name."

section.title: Step 6 — Analyze Results

rule.statement: Compute co-occurrence rates for all card pairs; apply thresholds: above 70% — group together; 40-70% — borderline; below 40% — keep separate.
rule.rationale: Co-occurrence data reveals which groupings are statistically robust vs. which reflect individual preferences. Outlier cards (below 40% with any partner) indicate content needing redesign, not just recategorization.

section.reference: Python script for computing co-occurrence matrix from open sort export
